title: Today's AI Brief: Kiwibit’s AI-powered bird feeder is my new backyard buddy
summary: A look at the latest AI news, featuring Kiwibit's AI-powered bird feeder and Boston Children's Hospital's use of AI for rare disease diagnosis.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Kiwibit’s AI-powered bird feeder is my new backyard buddy
imageCaption: Kiwibit’s AI-powered bird feeder is my new backyard buddy
imageSourceTitle: Kiwibit’s AI-powered bird feeder is my new backyard buddy

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's AI Brief. I've been spending some quality time with Kiwibit's AI-powered bird feeder, and I have to say, it's become my new favorite backyard buddy. This thing is like a smart home device, but for birds. It's got a 4K AI camera, and it's been fascinating to watch the birds interact with it.

The Kiwibit Bird Feeder Pro 4K AI Camera is more than just a novelty - it's actually pretty useful. The AI helps with bird identification, and it's been fun to see how accurately it can pick out different species. Plus, the setup was a breeze, and there are multiple mounting options, so you can place it just about anywhere. I've learned a lot about the birds that visit my yard, and it's amazing how much detail the camera captures.

In other news, Boston Children's Hospital is using OpenAI technology to improve patient care and diagnose rare diseases. According to reports, they've already used the tech to help diagnose over 40 rare disease cases. It's a great example of how AI can be used to make a real difference in people's lives. The intersection of AI and healthcare is really exciting, and it's amazing to see the impact it's having on patients and families.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
